params.text:
Conditions you may come across

You do not need clinical knowledge to support someone well, but a general awareness helps you notice when a person is struggling. Remember: these are things you might notice, not a checklist for diagnosis.

  • Anxiety: excessive worry, restlessness, a racing heart, avoiding situations, or seeking constant reassurance.
  • Depression: lasting low mood, loss of interest, tiredness, changes in appetite or sleep, and feelings of hopelessness.
  • Bipolar disorder: marked swings between very low periods and elevated, high-energy periods.
  • Psychosis: losing touch with shared reality — this can include hallucinations (sensing things others do not) or delusions (strongly held beliefs that are not based in fact).

Noticing and reporting

People express distress in different ways. Some withdraw and go quiet; others become agitated or tearful. A change from what is usual for that person often matters more than any single sign. You know the people you support — trust that knowledge.

  • Watch for change: a normally sociable person who stops joining in, or someone who suddenly cannot sleep.
  • Listen without fixing: you do not need answers; presence and attention help.
  • Record what you observe: note the facts, not your interpretation.
  • Report promptly: pass concerns to your senior, the nurse or the care plan so the right professional can act.

If someone talks about harming themselves or others, treat it seriously and escalate straight away. This is never something to keep to yourself.

params.question: A person you support usually enjoys the lunch club but has stopped attending, seems tearful and is not eating well. What is the most appropriate awareness-level response?

answers.text: Note the changes factually and report your concern to your senior or the care plan.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Recognising a change from what is usual, recording it and reporting it is exactly your role at awareness level.

answers.text: Tell the person they are showing signs of depression so they can get treatment.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Diagnosing is not your role. You recognise, respond and report — qualified professionals assess and diagnose.

answers.text: Wait a few weeks to see whether the low mood clears up by itself.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Delaying could leave the person without support they need. A clear change should be reported promptly.

answers.text: Encourage the person to cheer up and join in as normal.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This dismisses genuine distress. Listen and report rather than urging someone to simply feel better.
